_comment: Lars Demo Video - IJCAI 2026 Demo Track
_version: 13.0 - IJCAI timing cut: fast intro + compressed prompt engineering
_design_principles: Live collab with a real second browser
Dialogue style: Alex (Lars expert) explains, David (reviewer perspective) asks smart questions — viewer should identify with David

guest.name: David
guest.description: A reviewer persona who has read the paper and asks practical questions — the audience should identify with him

_tts_naming: TTS models say 'Lars' (without the leading L) because they mispronounce 'Lars'. In all written text, overlays, and UI, the correct name is 'Lars'.

steps._section: === INTRO (35s) — Fast problem framing and login ===
steps._concept: Alex and David introduce themselves. Alex states the core problem and triggers the merge animation exactly when saying Lars.
steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Hi, I'm Alex. Today I'm joined by my colleague David.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Hi, I'm David. I read the paper and I'm curious what you'll show me today.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: There is still a gap between quality goals and technical implementation in LLM projects. LARS solves this gap by bringing everyone into one shared workflow.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Sounds good, Alex. I'm following along.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Here's the Lars pipeline, which addresses this problem. Teams start in Prompt Engineering, where domain experts and developers co-author prompts together in real time. Those prompts feed into Batch Generation, which runs them across multiple models and datasets. And everything flows into Hybrid Evaluation, where humans and LLMs assess the outputs side by side — with live agreement metrics.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: We start in Prompt Engineering, where domain and technical experts work on one shared prompt.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: I'll create a fresh prompt and share it with a second reviewer account so we can edit it live together.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: First I name the prompt, then I add ijcai_reviewer_2 as collaborator in the share field.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Great, the prompt is shared now. David, please open a second browser and sign in as reviewer two. While you log in, I'll already create the first block.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Perfect. Now that you're in, I'll add the Task Explanation block.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Thanks for waiting, I can see the blocks.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly. I add the third block for the data format now.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Great, I now fill the blocks and add the Variable Subject. David, can you add the Content Variable in the same block.

actions.text: You are a counselling assistant that extracts key facts from psychosocial counselling communication.
Read the email thread and write a short situation summary in 2 to 3 sentences. Focus on family situation, health, and main concerns.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Sure, I add it now. I see, we can really edit this prompt together in real time.

actions.text:
}
The fields come from one counselling case: subject line and full message thread.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Exactly. I can see your update immediately. Now I add sample values so we can test.

actions.text:
Client wrote:
My 14-year-old son has refused school for three weeks and reports headaches. The teacher mentioned social exclusion.

Counsellor wrote:
School refusal can indicate stress and possible bullying. Arrange a meeting with the school counsellor and define next support steps.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Great. I click Test. Lars injects our variables into the prompt, we can choose the LLM in this dropdown, and then it streams the answer back live. Now let's scroll down and review the output carefully.

actions.after: choose the llm in this dropdown

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: The version panel tracks who changed what.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Perfect. We now have the live co-authored prompt and a second prepared prompt. We'll compare both in Batch Generation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: We've connected our own Mistral and Magistral models, but any user can bring their own. Let me add OpenAI Models step by step.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: First I select the type OpenAI, then paste the API key, then pick GPT-5 Nano and GPT-5 Mini, and finally click create.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Can I share this provider with my colleague so they can also use these models?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Absolutely — your API key stays private, but others can use the models. Let me share it with IJCAI Reviewer 2.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: So we have two prompts now. What's the next step — how do we actually run them against data?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: That's where Batch Generation comes in. We create a job that runs our prompts against a dataset with multiple models at once.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: First, the data: ten counselling cases in JSON. Lets look at the JSON structure, here ist the subject, and the corresponding content.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: I understand. This data is filled in our variables we just created, right? Let's set it up.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes, Lars maps those fields automatically. I'll paste the JSON into the wizard.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: And now we pick which prompts to use? I see both our prompts.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes you are right. Next we pick models. I'll take Mistral Small — one of our connected open-source models — and GPT-5 Nano from OpenAI, so we can compare providers directly.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: I'll name this job and we can review the full configuration.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Here I see the generation matrix, this is the Cartesian Product of our setup, right?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes, ten data points, times two prompts, times two models — forty outputs total. And notice the cost estimate — Lars calculates expected tokens and cost before you run anything.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: While that job runs in the background, let me open a completed batch so we can look at the actual outputs.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: I understand, here I see how it looks after the Batch Generation is done! Can we look at one of these outputs in Detail?

actions.after: look at one of these outputs

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes of course. Here's the full trace — rendered prompt blocks on the left, generated output on the right, plus metadata. The provenance is fully tracked — you always know which prompt and model produced each output.

actions.after: rendered prompt blocks on the left

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: OK — so we have forty situation descriptions now. Mistral versus GPT-5 Nano, each with two prompts. But how do I as a domain expert decide which combination actually works best for my use case?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Good Question. From a completed batch, we can open the Scenario Wizard right away.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: For cross-model comparison, we choose Ranking — specifically Bucket Ranking.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Bucket Ranking — so instead of sorting all four items into one long list, I just drop them into categories like Good, Medium, and Poor?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes you do. Evaluators place each item into one bucket, so they avoid forced full ordering.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: And below that, we configure assignment and randomization. Here you see Distribution Settings, then Distribution Mode, and finally Order Mode.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Great. So I can choose full overlap or split items across evaluators, and can decide if outputs are displayed in a random order?

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes, as you want. Next we add ourselves as human evaluators. For LLM-as-Evaluator, I'll pick Magistral and GPT-5 Mini. Once both are selected, we continue to the overview.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: And we create the scenario. We actually set one up earlier with nearly completed evaluations — but first, let me show you what evaluation looks like from the evaluator's perspective.

steps._section: === HUMAN EVALUATION (30s) — Show bucket ranking from evaluator perspective ===
steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: From the scenario workspace, I'll go back to Home and open the Evaluation module. In this evaluator view, each assigned scenario appears as a card.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: These are the ten counselling cases? Each with four situation descriptions to rank. Ah, I see those are the situation descriptions we just generated with the Batch Generation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Here you see bucket ranking, the default evaluation mode in Lars. Instead of a full ordering, evaluators drag items into quality categories. Notice there are no labels, so model and prompt identity stay hidden.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: That is much easier than sorting everything into one long list! I see, this is a completely blinded evaluation.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Yes, no model names, no prompt labels, and every evaluator sees items in a different random order. Unbiased by design. Now let's switch to the scenario owner's perspective and look at the aggregated results.

steps._section: === SCENARIO MANAGER (compact) — Evaluation Agreement Metrics + Provenance ===
steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: In Scenario Manager, this pre-built scenario already contains rankings from both human evaluators and two LLM evaluators.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: David, the Evaluation tab offers many metrics and diagnostics. To keep this demo short, we'll focus on provenance analysis.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Now let's focus on provenance. At the top, we see top-bucket hits by LLM, by prompt, and by prompt plus LLM pair. In the Best Prompt and LLM Pair card, the first number is how often this pair landed in the Good bucket, and the second number is how many rankings are completed so far. One evaluator still has open rankings, so these totals are not final yet and update live as new rankings come in. Now let's scroll down. Here are the three bucket-distribution plots. The first plot is the bucket distribution for the LLMs, the second plot is the bucket distribution for the prompts, and the third plot is the bucket distribution for the pairs.

actions.after: best prompt and llm pair card

actions.code: (function(){ const el = document.querySelector('.provenance-best-grid .provenance-best-card:nth-child(3) .provenance-best-meta'); if(!el) return false; const raw = (el.textContent || '').trim(); const m = raw.match(/^(.*\|\s*)(\d+)\s*\/\s*(\d+)(.*)$/); if(!m) return false; el.innerHTML = `${m[1]}${m[2]}/${m[3]}${m[4] || ''}`; return true; })();

actions.after: first number is how often this pair landed in the good bucket
second number is how many rankings are completed so far
update live as new rankings come in
first plot is the bucket distribution for the llms
second plot is the bucket distribution for the prompts
third plot is the bucket distribution for the pairs

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: We used counselling data here, but the workflow is domain-agnostic and transfers directly to other text domains.

steps.speaker: guest
steps.narration: Thanks Alex! Great walkthrough. I'll log in again and explore it myself.

steps.speaker: moderator
steps.narration: Thanks David. Live URL, credentials, and repository are on screen.
